title: Starving and Aging
content:
T144d,

Starvation in Process Scheduling

Starvation in process scheduling occurs when a process is repeatedly denied the resources it requires because other processes are prioritized. This typically happens in priority-based scheduling systems, where processes with higher priority keep arriving, causing lower-priority processes to be delayed indefinitely.

Starvation as a Problem

Starvation can be a critical issue in scheduling because it leads to inefficient system performance. When certain processes are never executed, it can make the system unstable and leave tasks unfinished. This can disrupt the operation of time-sensitive tasks and compromise the fairness of resource allocation.

Ageing as a Solution to the Starvation Problem

Ageing is a method used to combat starvation by gradually increasing the priority of a process the longer it waits in the queue. This ensures that processes with lower priority will eventually gain a higher priority, allowing them to execute after a reasonable amount of time. Ageing helps maintain balance within the system by making sure that no process waits indefinitely for resources.

With ageing, the system ensures that all processes get a fair chance to execute. As processes wait longer in the queue, their priority increases, making them more likely to be scheduled for execution. This prevents long delays for low-priority processes and maintains the fairness of resource distribution.

Ageing in Process Scheduling

Ageing is commonly implemented in systems where processes have assigned priorities. As a process waits, its priority is periodically boosted to ensure it eventually gets executed, even if higher-priority processes continue to arrive.

Starvation and Ageing in Different Scheduling Algorithms

1. First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)

FCFS is generally free from starvation because processes are handled in the order they arrive. However, it can experience a convoy effect, where short processes are delayed behind long processes. In such cases, shorter processes may wait unnecessarily long, but no process is completely starved.

2. Shortest Job Next (SJN)

SJN can lead to starvation for longer processes because shorter jobs are always prioritized. In this scenario, ageing can be used to gradually increase the priority of the longer processes to allow them to eventually execute.

3. Priority Scheduling

Priority scheduling is especially prone to starvation, as low-priority processes may never be scheduled if higher-priority processes keep arriving. In such systems, ageing is often applied to gradually increase the priority of waiting processes, ensuring that they get their turn to execute.

4. Round Robin (RR)

Round Robin scheduling typically prevents starvation since each process is allocated a fixed time slice in a cycle. As a result, every process gets CPU time and is prevented from being indefinitely delayed. Therefore, ageing is less necessary in Round Robin systems, but can still be used if needed for optimization.

Real-world Examples

Example 1: Multi-user Operating System

In a multi-user operating system, imagine a background system maintenance process with lower priority compared to user applications. If user applications continuously arrive, the maintenance process might starve and never get CPU time. With ageing, the priority of the maintenance process gradually increases, ensuring it eventually gets the CPU time it needs, even when other processes keep arriving.

Example 2: Network Packet Scheduling

In network packet scheduling, low-priority packets might starve if high-priority packets keep arriving. Ageing helps ensure that all packets, regardless of priority, eventually get transmitted. As low-priority packets age, their priority increases, allowing them to eventually get processed.

Preventing Starvation in Scheduling

To prevent starvation in scheduling systems, one of the most effective methods is ageing. By gradually increasing the priority of waiting processes, all tasks are eventually given a fair opportunity to execute.

Round Robin scheduling also naturally avoids starvation by allocating fixed time slices to each process, ensuring no process is neglected.

Other techniques to prevent starvation include setting a maximum wait time for processes or using feedback queues. In feedback queues, processes move between different priority levels based on their waiting time or service needs. This ensures that processes do not remain in low-priority queues indefinitely, providing a balanced and fair distribution of resources.
